chunk_summary: Guidelines for transitioning SAM patients from F-75 to F-100 feeding, emphasizing safety, monitoring for complications, and achieving catch-up growth while preventing refeeding syndrome.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for transitioning from F-75 to F-100 feeding in SAM management.
Identify warning signs of cardiac intolerance during rehabilitation feeding.
Learn to monitor and interpret catch-up growth rates in SAM patients.
Recognize and prevent refeeding syndrome during nutritional rehabilitation.
key_facts: Transition to F-100 feeding should begin only when appetite has returned, temperature is stable, and oedema is reducing or resolved.
A practical schedule involves replacing F-75 with an equal volume of F-100 for 2 days, then gradually increasing feed amounts.
Daily weight gain is categorized as good (≥10 g/kg/day), moderate (5 to <10 g/kg/day), and poor (<5 g/kg/day).
Warning signs of cardiac intolerance include tachypnea, rising pulse, hepatomegaly, and recurrent oedema.
Refeeding syndrome can cause arrhythmias, weakness, and respiratory failure; prevention includes phased feeding and electrolyte monitoring.
reference_quote: Begin rehabilitation only when appetite has returned, temperature is stable, there has been no further hypoglycemia, and oedema is reducing or resolved. (Source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, 2011)
document_key_topics: Transition from F-75 to F-100 feeding
